tool.name: aoptk find relationships chemicals
tool.description: Identify relationships between chemicals and effects.

configfiles.configfile:
import os import sys from pathlib import Path import pandas as pd import yaml from aoptk.literature.pdf import PDF from aoptk.literature.pymupdf_parser import PymupdfParser from aoptk.effect import Effect from aoptk.chemical import Chemical from aoptk.relationships.relationship_type import Causative from aoptk.relationships.relationship_type import Inhibitive from aoptk.text_generation_api import TextGenerationAPI
def init_relationships_output(): relationships_path = get_relationships_path(publication_id) relationships_dir = os.path.dirname(relationships_path) if relationships_dir: os.makedirs(relationships_dir, exist_ok=True) with open(relationships_path, "w") as f_out: f_out.write("id\tchemical\teffect\trelationship\n")
def write_relationships(publication_id, relationships): with open(get_relationships_path(publication_id), "a") as f_out: for relationship in relationships: f_out.write(f"{publication_id}\t{relationship.chemical}\t{relationship.effect}\t{relationship.relationship_type}\n")
def init_chemicals_output(): chemicals_path = get_chemicals_path(publication_id) chemicals_dir = os.path.dirname(chemicals_path) if chemicals_dir: os.makedirs(chemicals_dir, exist_ok=True) with open(chemicals_path, "w") as f_out: f_out.write("id\tchemical\n")
def write_chemicals(publication_id, chemicals): with open(get_chemicals_path(publication_id), "a") as f_out: for chemical in chemicals: chemical_name = chemical.name if hasattr(chemical, "name") else chemical f_out.write(f"{publication_id}\t{chemical_name}\n")
def get_relationships_path(publication_id): #if $input_source.input_mode == "single" return "relationships.tsv" #else return f"relationships/{publication_id}.tsv" #end if
def get_chemicals_path(publication_id): #if $input_source.input_mode == "single" return "chemicals.tsv" #else return f"chemicals/{publication_id}.tsv" #end if
@MODEL_SETUP@
text_generation = TextGenerationAPI(model=model, api_key=litellm_api_key, url=litellm_base_url)
#if $input_source.input_mode == "single" text_inputs = [("$input_source.input_file", "$input_source.input_file.element_identifier")] #else text_inputs = [ #for $text_file in $input_source.input_collection ("$text_file", "$text_file.element_identifier"), #end for ] #end if
#if $input_source.analysis_purpose.method == "relationships_chemicals" effects = [] #for $effect in $input_source.analysis_purpose.effects effects.append(Effect("$effect.effect")) #end for
relationship_types = [] types = [t.strip() for t in "$input_source.analysis_purpose.relationship_types".split(",")] for type in types: if type == "causative": relationship_types.append(Causative()) elif type == "inhibitive": relationship_types.append(Inhibitive()) else: raise ValueError(f"Unsupported relationship type: {type}")
for input_path, element_identifier in text_inputs: with open(input_path, "r") as f_in: text = f_in.read() publication_id = os.path.splitext(element_identifier)[0] init_relationships_output() init_chemicals_output() chemicals = text_generation.find_chemicals(text) relationships = text_generation.find_relationships_in_text(text=text, chemicals=chemicals, effects=effects, relationship_types=relationship_types) write_relationships(publication_id, relationships) write_chemicals(publication_id, chemicals)
#elif $input_source.analysis_purpose.method == "relationships" effects = [] #for $effect in $input_source.analysis_purpose.effects effects.append(Effect("$effect.effect")) #end for
relationship_types = [] types = [t.strip() for t in "$input_source.analysis_purpose.relationship_types".split(",")] for type in types: if type == "causative": relationship_types.append(Causative()) elif type == "inhibitive": relationship_types.append(Inhibitive()) else: raise ValueError(f"Unsupported relationship type: {type}")
#if $input_source.input_mode == "single" chemical_inputs = ["$input_source.analysis_purpose.chemicals"] #else chemical_inputs = [ #for $chemical_file in $input_source.analysis_purpose.chemicals "$chemical_file", #end for ] #end if
if len(text_inputs) != len(chemical_inputs): raise ValueError("Text and chemical collections must contain the same number of elements")
for (input_path, element_identifier), chemicals_path in zip(text_inputs, chemical_inputs): with open(input_path, "r") as f_in: text = f_in.read() publication_id = os.path.splitext(element_identifier)[0] init_relationships_output() chemicals = [Chemical(chem) for chem in pd.read_csv(chemicals_path, sep="\t")["chemical"].tolist()] relationships = text_generation.find_relationships_in_text(text=text, chemicals=chemicals, effects=effects, relationship_types=relationship_types) write_relationships(publication_id, relationships)
#elif $input_source.analysis_purpose.method == "chemicals" for input_path, element_identifier in text_inputs: with open(input_path, "r") as f_in: text = f_in.read() publication_id = os.path.splitext(element_identifier)[0] init_chemicals_output() chemicals = text_generation.find_chemicals(text) write_chemicals(publication_id, chemicals) #end if

param.name: input_mode
param.label: Input type
param.help: Choose whether to analyze one text file or every text file in a collection.

param.name: method
param.label: Purpose of the analysis.
param.option: Identify relationships, provide your own chemicals

param.name: input_collection
param.label: Texts to analyze
param.help: Input collection of text files (abstracts/publications/parsed pdfs).

param.name: method
param.label: Purpose of the analysis.
param.option: Identify relationships, provide your own chemicals

param.name: chemicals
param.label: Chemicals
param.help: Input collection of TSV files with chemicals.

tool.help:
Identify Relationships Between Chemicals and Biological Effects ===============================================================
This tool extracts chemical entities from text or leverages a user-defined chemical list, then maps relationships between those chemicals and a specified effect.
Additional Information ---------------------- - **Effect**: Simply specify the effect you want to analyze (e.g., liver fibrosis). - **Relationship Type**: Choose whether you want to identify causative (e.g., chemical causes / does not cause liver fibrosis) or inhibitive relationships (e.g., chemical inhibits / does not inhibit gap juction intracellular communication). - **Prompt specification**: The LLM is preconfigured to identify relationships between chemicals and biological effects. You can further refine the analysis by specifying additional constraints—such as limiting results to a particular measurement method or experimental setup. Simply describe your requirements in the prompt, and the model will try to tailor the output accordingly.
